Waste Type: Real NdFeB magnet leachate (aqueous solution derived from permanent Nd–Fe–B magnets; selective leaching used to remove Fe/B/Dy/Co interference)
Metals Tested: Nd (50–250 mg/L; stock standardized to 1000 mg/L Nd); Fe, B, Dy, Co analyzed but reported below detection limit in prepared solutions
Removal Efficiency Pct: Nd: % removal not tabulated; >90% of Nd biosorption occurred before equilibrium (equilibrium reached within ~30 min across temperatures)
Compliance Status: Not assessed against discharge limits (not mentioned in source).
Limiting Factor: pH control and (in general) Fe competition/precipitation: Fe can precipitate as pH rises, reducing competition; however Fe was removed by selective leaching so competitive effects were not present in tested leachate
Contact Time: Up to 90 min (samples taken to 90 min); equilibrium within ~30 min
Kinetic Isotherm Fit: Pseudo-second-order kinetics (R²>0.99); Langmuir isotherm (qmax 188.68 mg/g; KL 0.029 L/mg)

Waste Type: Mixed leachate solution derived from neodymium magnets (batch and continuous sorption systems)
Metals Tested: Nd (initial 250 mg/L)
Removal Efficiency Pct: Not mentioned in source (reported as uptake capacity, e.g., q = 157.21 mg/g at pH 5).
Compliance Status: Not assessed against discharge limits (not mentioned in source).
Limiting Factor: Slow Nd sorption kinetics reduced capacity in continuous vs batch operation
Contact Time: Not reported (described qualitatively as slow kinetics)
Kinetic Isotherm Fit: Not reported (q reported; kinetics described as slow)

Waste Type: Synthetic multi-metal solution (5 ppm each of Cu, La, Nd, Dy); tested at different pH
Metals Tested: Cu 5 ppm; La 5 ppm; Nd 5 ppm; Dy 5 ppm
Removal Efficiency Pct: Overall removal efficiency: <9% at pH 2.5; up to 86% at pH 5.0 (solution-wide metric; per-metal % not specified)
Final Concentration Mg L: Not mentioned in source (uptake reported as mg/g in some conditions).
Compliance Status: Not assessed against discharge limits (not mentioned in source).
Limiting Factor: Orthophosphate limits REE solubility/availability and thus biosorption under growth-compatible conditions (phosphate + high acidity)
Biomass Form: Living biomass (grown, centrifuged, washed, resuspended to OD 10)
Reactor Type: Batch (conical flasks / capped bottles; 30 mL reaction volume)
Contact Time: Samples at 3 h and 20 h
Kinetic Isotherm Fit: Not mentioned in source.

Waste Type: Synthetic La-spiked nutrient-replete growth medium (phosphate present)
Metals Tested: La added to reach 20 µM and 100 µM (depending on condition/pH)
Removal Efficiency Pct: No measurable La biosorption observed at initial pH 1.5 (under all growth conditions)
Compliance Status: Not assessed against discharge limits (not mentioned in source).
Limiting Factor: Orthophosphate limits REE solubility and availability for biosorption
Reactor Type: Batch culture flasks (100 mL cultures in conical flasks)
Contact Time: At least 6 days (duration stated; time-resolved removal not reported)
Kinetic Isotherm Fit: Not mentioned in source.

Waste Type: Synthetic REE solutions: REE nitrate salts dissolved in modified Bold’s Basal Medium (BBM)
Removal Efficiency Pct: REE³⁺ biosorption reported as almost complete (>99%) within the first hour (with additional detail for Eu/Sm/Nd time courses)
Final Concentration Mg L: Eu residual reported as 76 ppb after >1 day; Sm residual 1.52×10⁻³ mM in early samples (Nd residual not numerically specified)
Compliance Status: Not assessed against discharge limits (not mentioned in source).
Limiting Factor: Nutrient/starvation state affects performance (biosorption more effective in culture media than starving culture); mechanism not fully resolved
Biomass Form: Living biomass (biomass stayed alive during experiments; separated by centrifugation)
Reactor Type: Batch flask cultures (250 mL flasks with 150 mL medium)
Contact Time: Minutes to ≤1 h for >99% uptake depending on REE (e.g., Nd within 30 min; Eu within ~1 h)
Kinetic Isotherm Fit: Not reported (tracked by ICP-MS/LIBS; no model fit parameters given)

Waste Type: Synthetic REE solutions: REE nitrate salts dissolved in modified BBM
Removal Efficiency Pct: REE³⁺ biosorption reported as almost complete (>99%) within the first hour (with additional detail for Eu/Sm/Nd time courses)
Final Concentration Mg L: Eu residual reported as 76 ppb after >1 day; Sm residual 1.52×10⁻³ mM in early samples (Nd residual not numerically specified)
Compliance Status: Not assessed against discharge limits (not mentioned in source).
Limiting Factor: Nutrient/starvation state affects performance; mechanism not fully resolved
Biomass Form: Living biomass (biomass stayed alive during experiments; separated by centrifugation)
Reactor Type: Batch flask cultures (250 mL flasks with 150 mL medium)
Contact Time: Minutes to ≤1 h for >99% uptake depending on REE (e.g., Nd within 30 min; Eu within ~1 h)
Kinetic Isotherm Fit: Not reported (tracked by ICP-MS/LIBS; no model fit parameters given)

Waste Type: Synthetic REE solutions (single-metal screening at 10 mM; plus equimolar mixed-metal competition tests)
Metals Tested: REEs: Ce, Nd, Tb, La; competition tests: Ce in presence of Al, Pb, Ni, Zn (mixed-metal solutions)
Removal Efficiency Pct: Not reported as % removal; reported as adsorption capacity (e.g., 84.2–91.5 mg·g⁻¹, species-dependent)
Compliance Status: Not assessed against discharge limits (not mentioned in source).
Limiting Factor: Mixed-metal competition reduces REE uptake; at higher concentrations other metals can replace Ce on binding sites
Reactor Type: Batch tube incubation (2 mL solutions, shaking)
Contact Time: Standard incubation 3 h; kinetics sampled 2–60 min (rapid uptake reported)
Kinetic Isotherm Fit: Isotherms fitted (Langmuir/Freundlich); Langmuir reported as best correlation in this study

Waste Type: Synthetic REE solutions (single-metal screening at 10 mM; plus equimolar mixed-metal competition tests)
Metals Tested: REEs: Ce, Nd, Tb, La; competition tests: Ce in presence of Al, Pb, Ni, Zn (mixed-metal solutions)
Removal Efficiency Pct: Not reported as % removal; reported as adsorption capacity (mg·g⁻¹) across tested conditions
Compliance Status: Not assessed against discharge limits (not mentioned in source).
Limiting Factor: Mixed-metal competition reduces REE uptake; other metals can replace Ce at higher total concentrations
Reactor Type: Batch tube incubation (2 mL solutions, shaking)
Contact Time: Standard incubation 3 h; kinetics sampled 2–60 min
Kinetic Isotherm Fit: Isotherms fitted (Langmuir/Freundlich); Langmuir reported as best correlation

Waste Type: Synthetic single-metal solutions (Nd³⁺/Eu³⁺ and other metals as individual solutions; stock solutions 10 mM)
Metals Tested: Single-metal solutions of Nd³⁺, Eu³⁺ and others: Au³⁺, Pb²⁺, Pt⁴⁺, Fe²⁺, Cu²⁺, Ni²⁺ (10 mM stocks; specific concentration series for Nd in isotherm tests)
Removal Efficiency Pct: Not reported as % removal; Nd sorption capacity reported (Qmax Nd ≈ 0.47±0.01 mmol·g⁻¹)
Compliance Status: Not assessed against discharge limits (not mentioned in source).
Limiting Factor: Nickel identified as least effectively bound ion (potential limiting/competing contaminant in mixed wastewaters)
Reactor Type: Batch sorption assays (biomass grown in shake flasks/bubble columns/15-L airlift reactor; sorption tests in lab vessels)
Contact Time: 3 h (Nd experiments) and 24 h (some Qmax experiments) depending on test
Biomass Dosage G L: 5–20 g/L tested; commonly 5 g/L (10 mg in 2 mL)
Kinetic Isotherm Fit: Isotherm fitting used (Langmuir/Freundlich); Langmuir-type behavior reported for some species in the study (kinetic models not reported)

Waste Type: Synthetic single-metal solutions (Nd³⁺/Eu³⁺ and other metals as individual solutions; stock solutions 10 mM)
Metals Tested: Single-metal solutions of Nd³⁺, Eu³⁺ and others: Au³⁺, Pb²⁺, Pt⁴⁺, Fe²⁺, Cu²⁺, Ni²⁺ (10 mM stocks; Nd isotherm series also reported)
Removal Efficiency Pct: Not reported as % removal; Nd sorption capacity reported (Qmax Nd ≈ 0.37 mmol·g⁻¹)
Compliance Status: Not assessed against discharge limits (not mentioned in source).
Limiting Factor: Nickel identified as least effectively bound ion; common wastewater metals (Pb/Fe/Cu/Ni) noted as potentially problematic competitors requiring pre-removal in application contexts
Reactor Type: Batch sorption assays (biomass grown in shake flasks/bubble columns/15-L airlift reactor; sorption tests in lab vessels)
Contact Time: 3 h (Nd experiments) and 24 h (some Qmax experiments) depending on test
Biomass Dosage G L: 5–20 g/L tested; commonly 5 g/L (10 mg in 2 mL)
Kinetic Isotherm Fit: Nd isotherm for Chlorella kessleri better fitted with Freundlich isotherm (kinetic models not reported)

Waste Type: Synthetic single-metal dysprosium solutions (Dy(NO3)2·6H2O; batch lab experiments)
Removal Efficiency Pct: Max Dy removal 50% at pH 3.0 during 1 h; fast initial removal 38% within 5 min
Compliance Status: Not assessed against discharge limits (not mentioned in source).
Limiting Factor: pH strongly affects removal capacity; increased pH beyond optimum reduced removal capacity
Contact Time: 5–120 min tested (max removal reported at 60 min)
Kinetic Isotherm Fit: Isotherm: Langmuir; kinetics: pseudo-second-order and Elovich (qmax Spirulina 3.24 mg/g)

Waste Type: Synthetic multi-contaminant water modeled on Loa River: B/As/Cu/Mn/Zn mixture (batch tests)
Metals Tested: B 60 mg/L; As 12 mg/L; Cu 3 mg/L; Mn 3 mg/L; Zn 3 mg/L
Removal Efficiency Pct: B removal in multimetal solution decreased markedly (reported as low as 0.2% for Chlamydomonas reinhardtii in multimetallic solutions)
Compliance Status: Not assessed against discharge limits (not mentioned in source).
Limiting Factor: Boron was the lowest yield ratio (YR) after 3 h, suggesting strong interference by other metals in multimetal systems (except Chlorophyceae spp.)
Biomass Form: Living biomass used in uptake experiments (biomass obtained after centrifugation; initial biomass concentration given)
Contact Time: Samples collected at 10 min and 3 h (extended sampling also reported at 24 h and 72 h)
Kinetic Isotherm Fit: No kinetic/isotherm model fits reported; rapid initial decrease within first 10 min noted

Waste Type: Synthetic multi-contaminant water modeled on Loa River: B/As/Cu/Mn/Zn mixture (batch tests)
Metals Tested: B 60 mg/L; As 12 mg/L; Cu 3 mg/L; Mn 3 mg/L; Zn 3 mg/L
Removal Efficiency Pct: B removal decreased from 21.4% (mono) to 6.6% (multi) for Chlorella vulgaris; metals like Mn can reach very high removals under some conditions (e.g., 99.4% for Mn at pH 7.0, 3 h)
Compliance Status: Not assessed against discharge limits (not mentioned in source).
Limiting Factor: Boron shows the lowest YR in multimetal systems (competition/interference)
Biomass Form: Living biomass used in uptake experiments (initial biomass concentration given)
Contact Time: Samples collected at 10 min and 3 h (also reported at 24 h and 72 h)
Kinetic Isotherm Fit: No kinetic/isotherm model fits reported; rapid initial decrease within first 10 min noted

Waste Type: Synthetic multi-contaminant water modeled on Loa River: B/As/Cu/Mn/Zn mixture (batch tests)
Metals Tested: B 60 mg/L; As 12 mg/L; Cu 3 mg/L; Mn 3 mg/L; Zn 3 mg/L
Removal Efficiency Pct: Reported maximum removals include B up to 38.6% (at pH 5.5, 10 min) and As up to 40.7% (at pH 9.5, 3 h) for Scenedesmus almeriensis under selected conditions
Compliance Status: Not assessed against discharge limits (not mentioned in source).
Limiting Factor: Boron adsorption is strongly affected by co-metals; multimetal competition reduces B uptake across microalgae tested
Contact Time: 10 min and 3 h reported for maximum removals (metal/species dependent)
Kinetic Isotherm Fit: Not mentioned in source.

Species: Chlorophyceae spp. (indigenous strain from polluted environment)
Waste Type: Synthetic multi-contaminant water modeled on Loa River: B/As/Cu/Mn/Zn mixture (batch tests)
Metals Tested: As, B, Cu, Mn, Zn (mixture); initial concentrations not specified in this summary source
Removal Efficiency Pct: Reported maximum removals include Zn up to 91.9% (pH 5.5, 3 h) and Cu up to 88% (pH 7.0, 10 min) for Chlorophyceae spp. under selected conditions
Compliance Status: Not assessed against discharge limits (not mentioned in source).
Limiting Factor: B removal efficiencies decreased markedly in multimetal solutions for many microalgae (exception noted for Chlorophyceae spp.)
Contact Time: 10 min and 3 h reported for maximum removals (metal dependent)
Kinetic Isotherm Fit: Not mentioned in source.

Waste Type: Real industrial wastewaters (South Africa): mine beneficiation plant wastewater; smelter tailings/return waters; plus simulated La/Tl solutions
Metals Tested: Industrial samples included: Co 0.316 mg/L (mine beneficiation); Tl 2.386 mg/L (smelter tailings); Cr 135.56 mg/L (smelter return water); simulated La 25–150 mg/L and Tl 150–800 mg/L also tested
Removal Efficiency Pct: Co: complete removal (100%) from 0.316 mg/L; Tl: 2.386→0.021 mg/L (~99%); Cr reduced but remained high (135.56→45.67 mg/L)
Final Concentration Mg L: Tl final 0.021 mg/L; Cr final 45.67 mg/L
Compliance Status: Not assessed against discharge limits (not mentioned in source).
Limiting Factor: Potential desorption behavior for Tl (reduction then desorption back over time) and binding-site saturation limiting further reduction
Biomass Form: Dried biomass (harvested, cleaned, oven-dried at 50°C overnight)
Contact Time: Kinetic sampling 5–1440 min; industrial sample contact reported as 24 h
Biomass Dosage G L: 1.0 g per 100 mL (≈1.0 g/L) used for waste samples
Kinetic Isotherm Fit: Langmuir performed better than Freundlich (linearized); kinetic modeling used non-linear Langmuir–Hinshelwood (AQUASIM) for La/Tl system

Waste Type: Real industrial wastewaters (South Africa) and simulated La/Tl solutions
Metals Tested: Simulated La 25–150 mg/L; simulated Tl 150–800 mg/L; industrial sample example: K 2036 mg/L (initial) reduced to 744.7 mg/L
Removal Efficiency Pct: K reduced from 2036 to 744.7 mg/L (~63%); La sorption capacity reported as qmax 143 mg/g (not % removal)
Compliance Status: Not assessed against discharge limits (not mentioned in source).
Limiting Factor: Binding site saturation can limit further reduction; Tl behavior (desorption over time) noted as an issue in the overall study context
Biomass Form: Dried biomass used in biosorption experiments (harvested, cleaned, oven-dried at 50°C)
Contact Time: Kinetic sampling between 5–1440 min; some batch contacts reported up to 24 h
Biomass Dosage G L: 1.0 g per 100 mL (≈1.0 g/L) used for waste samples (study also used 0.08 g per 150 mL in some tests; not standardized across all runs)
Kinetic Isotherm Fit: Langmuir fit preferred over Freundlich; La/Tl kinetics modeled using Langmuir–Hinshelwood equation in AQUASIM

Waste Type: Synthetic boron-spiked water (“B stress” experiments at multiple B levels)
Removal Efficiency Pct: Not mentioned in source.

Biomass Form: Living culture (biomass reached up to 4.5 g/L)
Contact Time: Not mentioned in source.
Biomass Dosage G L: Biomass concentration reached up to 4.5 g/L (culture concentration; dosing strategy not specified)
Kinetic Isotherm Fit: Not mentioned in source.

Waste Type: Synthetic single-metal cobalt solution added to photobioreactors (Co(NO3)2·6H2O; ~60–63.6 mg/L Co²⁺)
Metals Tested: Co²⁺ initial 63.6 mg/L (reported); also described as 60 mg/L added in methods
Final Concentration Mg L: Co²⁺: 2.49 mg/L at 30 min; 1.01 mg/L at 10 h
Compliance Status: Not assessed against discharge limits (synthetic solution; compliance not discussed)
Limiting Factor: Cobalt toxicity impacted viability (severe cell morphology deterioration); performance in real mixed wastewater matrices not tested
Biomass Form: Living culture (modified ‘endo’ medium; biomass concentrations in mg/L range)
Reactor Type: Glass tubular air-lift photobioreactor system (500 mL vessels)
Contact Time: Sampling at 30 min and 2–10 h (key results at 30 min and 10 h)
Biomass Dosage G L: Biomass concentrations used: 0.9–3.6 mg/L (0.0009–0.0036 g/L) and 4.2 mg/L stock reported
Kinetic Isotherm Fit: Not mentioned in source.
